---
title: "Deep Learning 2026: Technical Paths and Application Domains"
author: "Alexander D'hoore"
format:
  pptx:
    reference-doc: template-deep-learning.pptx
    slide-level: 2
    monofont: "Cousine"
---

# Welcome to Deep Learning

## Today’s Goal

- This short introduction gives you a **map of the field**.
- Not deep theory, but a practical overview of what kinds of deep-learning systems you could build.
- The main goal today:
  - choose a technical path
  - choose an application domain
  - start shaping your own project

![](media/generative-ai.png)

## How This Part of the Course Works

- The course is short:
  - 4 lessons of 4 hours
  - plus the exam
- The first lesson already introduced you to practical generative AI.
- The remaining lessons are mainly for building your own project.
- So today is about **choosing a good path and domain**.

## How You Will Be Evaluated

- You will build a **unique AI application or prototype**.
- At the exam, you will:
  - present what you built
  - explain your design choices
  - defend your code and architecture
- You may use AI tools while building.
- But during the exam, **you** must understand what your system does.

# How to Choose a Project

## First Choose a Technical Path

- Before choosing an application, it is often easier to choose a **technical path**.
- In this course, the main paths are:
  - train or retrain your own model
  - build with pretrained foundation models
  - adapt / fine-tune large pretrained models
  - reinforcement learning
  - edge AI and deployment

## Then Choose an Application Domain

- After that, choose the domain where you want to apply that path.
- Good domains include:
  - computer vision
  - text / LLM apps
  - multimodal and document AI
  - semantic search / retrieval
  - recommendation systems
  - speech and audio
  - time-series and sensor signals
  - 3D / depth / geometry
- Reinforcement learning and edge deployment also have dedicated notes in `domains/`.

## A Good Project Formula

> Input -> AI model -> useful output -> interface

Examples:

- image -> detector -> labels and boxes -> web app
- user question + documents -> retrieval + LLM -> answer -> chat UI
- speech -> transcription -> summary -> web app
- sensor data -> anomaly detector -> warning -> dashboard

## Scope Matters

- Pick something interesting, but also feasible.
- A good project usually has:
  - one clear problem
  - one main framework
  - one demoable result
- Avoid choosing:
  - the hardest model training path
  - and the hardest deployment path
  - at the same time

# Technical Paths

## Path 1: Train or Retrain Your Own Model

- This is the classical deep-learning workflow.
- Typical flow: dataset, model, training, evaluation, deployment.
- You may start from random weights or pretrained weights.
- Example: continue training MobileNet or YOLO on your own data.

## Path 1: Why You Might Choose It

- Good if you want to understand how model training really works.
- Strong domains: vision, audio, time-series, classification.
- Good tools: PyTorch, torchvision, torchaudio, YOLO, RF-DETR.
- Good projects: classifier, detector, anomaly detector.

## Path 2: Build With Pretrained Foundation Models

- Here the main work is **building around a model**, not training it yourself.
- You choose an existing model and turn it into an application.
- This is one of the most common modern AI workflows.
- Strong path for text, speech, multimodal systems, and generation.

## Path 2: Where To Explore

- Hugging Face is one of the best places to browse models, tasks, and examples.
- Model cards help you understand what a model can do.
- Good tools: Transformers, Diffusers, Sentence Transformers, Ollama.
- Good projects: chatbot, speech tool, image generator, document assistant.

## Path 3: Adapt or Fine-Tune Large Models

- Choose this path if you want to adapt a large pretrained model to your own task or style.
- This is more than prompting, but less than full retraining.
- For large models, retraining everything is often too expensive.
- Instead, you adapt part of the model.
- Common methods: LoRA, PEFT, adapters.

## Path 3: Concrete Example

- Example: fine-tune an image model on your own face or visual style.
- Example: adapt an LLM to a narrow domain.
- Good tools: Transformers training stack, PEFT, Diffusers, PyTorch.
- This path is powerful if you want to go beyond simple inference.

## Path 4: Reinforcement Learning

- This path is very different from supervised learning.
- The model learns by interacting with an environment.
- It tries actions, gets rewards, and improves over time.
- The environment can be a game, simulation, or physical system.

## Path 4: Why It Is Interesting

- The system discovers strategies by itself instead of copying labels.
- Good projects: game agent, simulator, small control task.
- Good tools: Gymnasium, Stable-Baselines3, TorchRL.
- This path works best when you keep the environment and goal simple.

## Path 5: Edge AI and Deployment

- Most of you will run models on your GPU server.
- Edge AI means running the model somewhere else locally.
- Examples: laptop, Jetson, Raspberry Pi, embedded device.
- The challenge is export, optimization, latency, and runtime choice.

## Path 5: Why It Matters

- Good if you like systems, deployment, and performance engineering.
- You can focus on making an existing model run well.
- Good tools: ONNX Runtime, JetPack, TensorRT, Ollama, llama.cpp.
- Strong examples: deploy vision, compare runtimes, run a local LLM.

# Application Domains

## What Application Domains Mean

- A technical path tells you **how** you work with models.
- An application domain tells you **what kind of data or problem** you work on.
- One domain can combine with several paths.
- Example: computer vision can use Path 1, Path 2, or Path 5.

## Domain 1: Computer Vision

- AI for images and video.
- Typical tasks:
  - classification
  - object detection
  - segmentation
  - depth estimation
  - keypoint detection
- Good tools:
  - PyTorch
  - YOLO
  - RF-DETR
  - OpenCV

## Computer Vision: Why It Matters

- This is an established field, but still extremely powerful.
- It solves many real industrial problems very well.
- It also works very well on the GPU hardware you have.
- Strong examples: inspection, counting, tracking, defect detection.

## Domain 2: Language Models and Text Systems

- This domain is mostly about pretrained or adapted models.
- Training your own LLM from scratch is usually not realistic.
- Typical tasks:
  - chatbot
  - summarization
  - classification
  - question answering
  - tool calling
- Good tools:
  - Ollama
  - Transformers
  - Sentence Transformers

## Language Models: Tool Calling and Apps

- Tool calling means the model can decide when to use another tool.
- Example tools:
  - search
  - calculator
  - database lookup
  - API call
- That can become a whole project by itself.

## Domain 3: Multimodal and Document AI

- This is where language meets images, documents, or audio.
- Typical tasks:
  - visual question answering
  - document understanding
  - receipt or invoice reading
  - image-aware assistants
- Good tools:
  - Transformers
  - vision-capable local models
  - document tools such as docTR
- You already saw a small example of this in the generative AI workshop.

## Multimodal: Example Projects

- upload an image and ask questions about it
- analyze a receipt or invoice
- build a document question-answering tool
- combine voice input with image understanding

## Domain 4: Semantic Search and Retrieval

- This domain is based on embeddings.
- An embedding is a vector representation of text, image, or audio.
- Similar meaning leads to nearby vectors.
- Typical projects:
  - semantic search
  - retrieval-augmented chatbot
  - similar-item finder

## Semantic Search: Why It Works

- You do not search only by exact words.
- You search by meaning.
- This is useful for:
  - documents
  - notes
  - images
  - other media

## RAG: Retrieve First, Then Generate

- Retrieval-augmented generation is part of this domain.
- First retrieve the most relevant documents.
- Then send that context to the language model.
- Then generate the answer with better grounding.

## Domain 5: Recommendation Systems

- This domain is closely related to embeddings, but the user experience is different from search.
- Typical projects:
  - music or media recommender
  - product recommendation demo
  - similar-item recommender
  - recommendation UI based on embeddings

## Recommendation Systems: Why They Matter

- Recommendation is everywhere on the modern web.
- Modern recommenders often use embeddings and ranking.
- This can be a very strong project if you like music, media, or product ideas.

## Domain 6: Speech and Audio

- This domain can use Path 1, Path 2, or Path 3.
- Typical tasks:
  - speech-to-text
  - text-to-speech
  - sound classification
  - music generation
  - voice adaptation
- Good tools:
  - Whisper
  - torchaudio
  - Transformers
  - TTS libraries

## Speech and Audio: Example Projects

- voice note transcriber
- speech-based chatbot
- sound-event detector
- text-to-speech assistant
- music generation demo

## Domain 7: Time-Series and Sensor Signals

- This is more industrial and less flashy, but still important.
- Typical tasks:
  - anomaly detection
  - forecasting
  - sensor monitoring
- It is especially relevant if you already have an interesting dataset.
- For this course, it is possible, but probably less attractive than image, text, or audio.

## Domain 8: 3D, Depth, and Geometry

- This is a more niche domain, but it exists.
- Examples:
  - depth estimation
  - image-to-3D
  - text-to-3D
  - 3D generation workflows
- Some interesting pretrained models already exist here.

## Paths and Domains Can Be Combined

- Computer vision can use Path 1, Path 2, or Path 5.
- Language models usually fit Path 2 or Path 3.
- Audio can use Path 1, Path 2, or Path 3.
- Edge deployment can apply to many domains.

# Framework Suggestions

## Frameworks Depend on the Domain

- You do not need to memorize every framework from this talk.
- The exact framework depends on the domain you choose.
- After choosing a domain, read the matching notes and official docs.
- A few big names you will see often:
  - PyTorch
  - Transformers
  - Sentence Transformers

## Supporting Tools

- These are useful, but they are not the main AI choice:
  - Gradio
  - Streamlit
  - FastAPI
  - React or another custom frontend
  - Docker
- Most of you will probably build some kind of interface.
- Gradio is a very good default if you want to move fast.
- React gives more control, but also more complexity.
- See `04-supporting-tools.md` in the repo for a short overview of these options.

![](media/frontend-user-interfaces.jpg)

# Final Expectations

## What I Want to See

- a unique project
- a clear technical path
- one main framework stack
- visible effort
- a working demo or prototype
- understanding of the code and architecture

## Final Message

- Deep learning is a very broad field.
- You do not need to master all of it in this course.
- But I want you to leave this course with:
  - a better map of the field
  - experience building one real AI system
  - confidence that you can learn a new framework on your own

## What To Do Next

- choose a technical path
- choose an application domain
- read the matching domain note
- choose a framework stack
- sketch a project idea
- start building

# Good Luck

- Pick something you find genuinely interesting.
- Build something real.
- Learn your tools deeply enough to explain what you made.
